title: Raising chicks to point of lay
subtitle: The 22-week arc from day-old to first egg, for a small keeper with a brooder and an outdoor run.
excerpt: Day-old chicks from a hatchery need heat, clean water, starter feed, and protection from predators for the first eight weeks. After that, the management shifts but the work continues: juvenile birds need growers pellets, outdoor access, and a secure night roost. Point of lay arrives at around 20 to 22 weeks depending on breed and season. This covers the whole arc.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002). https://www.gov.uk
British Hen Welfare Trust chick-rearing guidance. https://www.bhwt.org.uk
Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, general brooding methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The age at which a pullet begins to lay eggs, typically 20 to 22 weeks for a commercial hybrid layer. Breed, daylength, and condition all affect the exact moment.
A young female chicken from hatch until her first moult, conventionally between 0 and 12 months old. A pullet at point of lay is often sold as a POL pullet.
The warm, enclosed space that substitutes for a mother hen in the first weeks of a chick's life. A heat plate mimics a hen's underside; a heat lamp heats the air above.
A pelleted or mash feed formulated for birds from around 6 weeks to point of lay, lower in calcium than layer pellets. High-calcium layer feed before lay damages the kidneys of juvenile birds.

projectSchedule.title: Chicks arrive
projectSchedule.body: Fill the brooder, set the heat plate to 35°C equivalent height, place chicks directly under the plate.

projectSchedule.title: First health check
projectSchedule.body: Check each chick: clear eyes, no pasty vent, active feeding and drinking.

projectSchedule.title: Heat plate height raised
projectSchedule.body: Raise the plate legs one notch so temperature under the plate is a few degrees cooler. Chicks should be spending time away from the plate.

projectSchedule.title: Off the heat plate
projectSchedule.body: At 5 weeks, fully feathered chicks should not need supplementary heat in a sheltered environment above 18°C. Move outdoors in a protected run if weather allows.

projectSchedule.title: First outdoor access
projectSchedule.body: If weather is settled and temperatures above 15°C, begin short supervised outdoor sessions. Electric poultry netting or a small covered ark keeps them safe from daytime predators.

projectSchedule.title: Switch to growers feed
projectSchedule.body: Replace chick crumb with growers pellets. Ensure grit is available free-choice.

projectSchedule.title: Watch for first signs of lay
projectSchedule.body: Combs redden, birds begin investigating the nest boxes, production may be close. Switch to layer pellets now.

projectSchedule.title: Point of lay reached
projectSchedule.body: First eggs expected from now for hybrid layers. Bantams and pure breeds may take longer.

content.text: Buying day-old chicks from a hatchery is the cheapest route to a laying flock but requires the most management in the early weeks. A

at around 20 to 22 weeks. The arc from day-old to first egg has three phases: heat-dependent brooding (weeks 1 to 5), transition to outdoor conditions (weeks 6 to 16), and the pre-lay phase (weeks 16 to 22).
Day-old chicks cannot regulate their body temperature. A heat plate

set at the right height so the birds fit snugly underneath is far safer than an overhead heat lamp, which presents a fire risk if it fails or falls. Fresh water must be available at all times: chicks dehydrate fast. Use a small-gauge waterer so they cannot fall in. Feed chick crumb, which is finely ground and medicated against coccidiosis in most commercial formulations.

Fully feathered chicks at five weeks can cope with outdoor temperatures above 15°C in dry conditions. Begin outdoor access in a small, secure run. Electric netting keeps daytime foxes out; the chicks need to be securely housed at night from the first week. Introduce grass and scratch gradually alongside the chick crumb rather than switching overnight.

at around ten weeks. Continue the layer feed switch at 18 weeks when combs start to colour up and the birds begin investigating nest boxes. Do not introduce layer pellets before the comb reddens: the high calcium content damages immature kidneys.

The first eggs are often small and misshapen: soft-shelled, double-yolked, or oddly proportioned. This is normal in the first few weeks as the laying machinery runs in. By the end of the third week of lay, a hybrid layer should be producing a regular daily egg and the shell quality will have settled.
